name: pvt

members.name: PVTConfig
members.signature: PVTConfig(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, variant: str = 'pvt_tiny', embed_dims: tuple[int, ...] = (64, 128, 320, 512), depths: tuple[int, ...] = (2, 2, 2, 2), num_heads: tuple[int, ...] = (1, 2, 5, 8), sr_ratios: tuple[int, ...] = (8, 4, 2, 1), mlp_ratios: tuple[float, ...] = (8.0, 8.0, 4.0, 4.0))

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, variant: str = 'pvt_tiny', embed_dims: tuple[int, ...] = (64, 128, 320, 512), depths: tuple[int, ...] = (2, 2, 2, 2), num_heads: tuple[int, ...] = (1, 2, 5, 8), sr_ratios: tuple[int, ...] = (8, 4, 2, 1), mlp_ratios: tuple[float, ...] = (8.0, 8.0, 4.0, 4.0))

members.summary: Configuration dataclass for every PVT (v2) variant.
members.extended:
`PVTConfig` is an immutable container that fully specifies the
architecture of a Pyramid Vision Transformer v2 (Wang et al., 2021
and 2022).  The implementation in `lucid.models.vision.pvt`
builds PVT v2 by default: overlapping convolutional patch
embedding at each stage, a depthwise-conv-augmented MLP, and the
Spatial-Reduction Attention (SRA) from PVT v1.

parameters.name: num_classes
parameters.description:
Number of output classes for the classification head.  Defaults
to `1000` (ImageNet-1k).

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of input image channels.  Defaults to `3` (RGB).

parameters.name: variant
parameters.description:
Informational label identifying the canonical variant
(`"pvt_v2_b0"` through `"pvt_v2_b5"`).  Defaults to
`"pvt_tiny"`.

parameters.name: embed_dims
parameters.description:
Output channel width per stage (4 stages).  Defaults to
`(64, 128, 320, 512)` — PVT v2-B1.

parameters.name: depths
parameters.description:
Number of transformer blocks per stage.  Defaults to
`(2, 2, 2, 2)`.

parameters.name: num_heads
parameters.description:
Number of attention heads per stage.  Defaults to
`(1, 2, 5, 8)`.

parameters.name: sr_ratios
parameters.description:
Spatial-reduction ratio $R_i$ per stage; `1` disables
SR for the deepest stage.  Defaults to `(8, 4, 2, 1)`.

parameters.name: mlp_ratios
parameters.description:
Per-stage MLP expansion ratio.  Defaults to
`(8.0, 8.0, 4.0, 4.0)` — early stages widen more than late
stages, following PVT v2-B1.

examples:
Build a PVT v2-B1 configuration with a 100-class head:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.pvt import PVTConfig
>>> cfg = PVTConfig(num_classes=100)
>>> cfg.embed_dims, cfg.depths, cfg.sr_ratios, cfg.num_classes
((64, 128, 320, 512), (2, 2, 2, 2), (8, 4, 2, 1), 100)
notes:
Spatial-Reduction Attention (SRA) reduces the per-stage attention
cost from $\mathcal{O}((HW)^2)$ to
$\mathcal{O}((HW)^2 / R_i^2)$ by downsampling the K / V
feature map by stride $R_i$ before computing attention.

Reference (v1): Wenhai Wang *et al.*, *"Pyramid Vision Transformer:
A Versatile Backbone for Dense Prediction without Convolutions"*,
ICCV 2021, [arXiv:2102.12122](https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.12122).
Reference (v2): Wenhai Wang *et al.*, *"PVT v2: Improved Baselines
with Pyramid Vision Transformer"*, CVMJ 2022,
[arXiv:2106.13797](https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.13797).

attributes.name: model_type
attributes.description: Constant string `"pvt"` used by the model registry.

members.name: PVT
members.extended:
The Pyramid Vision Transformer v2 is a pure-transformer backbone
that produces a *hierarchical* feature pyramid — making it a
drop-in replacement for ResNet-style backbones in dense prediction
tasks (detection, segmentation).  The trunk has four stages, each
beginning with an *overlapping* convolutional patch embedding
($7 \times 7$ stride 4 for stage 0; $3 \times 3$
stride 2 for subsequent stages).  Inside each stage every
transformer block applies *Spatial-Reduction Attention* (SRA):

$$
K, V \leftarrow \mathrm{LN}\!\bigl(
    W_R \cdot \mathrm{Reshape}_{R_i \times R_i}(x)\bigr),
\qquad
\mathrm{SRA}(x) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(
    \frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right) V,
$$

where $R_i$ decreases with depth (e.g. $8, 4, 2, 1$).
This cuts the per-stage attention cost from
$\mathcal{O}((HW)^2)$ to $\mathcal{O}((HW)^2 / R_i^2)$.
PVT v2 additionally inserts a depthwise $3 \times 3$
convolution between the two MLP linears, providing spatial mixing
inside the feed-forward sublayer and removing the need for
explicit positional embeddings.

`forward_features` returns the mean-pooled final-stage
feature $(B, \texttt{embed\_dims[-1]})$.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen dataclass specifying `embed_dims`, `depths`,
`num_heads`, `sr_ratios`, `mlp_ratios`, `in_channels`,
and `num_classes`.  See `PVTConfig`.

examples:
Build a PVT v2-B1 backbone and run a forward pass:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.pvt import PVT, PVTConfig
>>> model = PVT(PVTConfig())
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> feat = model.forward_features(x)
>>> feat.shape                       # (B, embed_dims[-1])
(1, 512)
notes:
Reference (v1): Wenhai Wang *et al.*, *"Pyramid Vision Transformer:
A Versatile Backbone for Dense Prediction without Convolutions"*,
ICCV 2021, [arXiv:2102.12122](https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.12122).
Reference (v2): Wenhai Wang *et al.*, *"PVT v2: Improved Baselines
with Pyramid Vision Transformer"*, CVMJ 2022.

attributes.name: patch_embed
attributes.description:
Top-level $7 \times 7$ stride-4 patch embedding feeding
stage 0.

attributes.name: stages
attributes.description:
Four `_PVTStage` modules; stages 1-3 each contain their
own $3 \times 3$ stride-2 patch-embedding downsampler.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Four-stage feature description with cumulative reductions
$(4, 8, 16, 32)$.

members.name: PVTForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: PVT v2 with a linear classification head (Wang et al., 2022).
members.extended:
Wraps the same hierarchical four-stage trunk as `PVT`
(overlapping patch embeddings + Spatial-Reduction Attention) and
adds a global mean pool + single `nn.Linear` classification
head:

$$
\text{logits} = W_{\text{head}}\,
    \mathrm{Mean}(z^{L}) + b_{\text{head}},
\qquad W_{\text{head}} \in
    \mathbb{R}^{C_{\text{out}} \times d_{L}}.
$$

Pass `labels` to `forward` to compute the cross-entropy
loss in the same pass.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture specification.  Must set `num_classes` to the
desired number of output categories.  See `PVTConfig`.

examples:
End-to-end inference with the default PVT v2-B1 classifier:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.pvt import (
...     PVTConfig, PVTForImageClassification,
... )
>>> model = PVTForImageClassification(PVTConfig())
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Reference: Wang *et al.*, *"PVT v2: Improved Baselines with
Pyramid Vision Transformer"*, CVMJ 2022.  PVT v2-B0 / B1 / B2 / B3
/ B4 / B5 reach **70.5 / 78.7 / 82.0 / 83.1 / 83.6 / 83.8 %
top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224 (Wang et al., 2022, Table 1).

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description:
Final linear projection of width
`(num_classes, embed_dims[-1])`.

members.name: pvt_tiny
members.extended:
Backwards-compatible alias kept for users on older releases that
referred to the ~14M-parameter variant as "PVT-Tiny".  Identical
to `pvt_v2_b1` in every respect.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k pretrained weights when
available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical PVT v2-B1 config.

returns.description:
A `PVT` backbone returning a flat $(B, 512)$
feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.pvt import pvt_tiny
>>> model = pvt_tiny()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 512)

see_also.name: pvt_v2_b1
see_also.description: The canonical factory this alias forwards to.

members.name: pvt_tiny_cls
members.extended:
Backwards-compatible alias kept for users on older releases that
referred to the ~14M-parameter classifier as "PVT-Tiny".  Identical
to `pvt_v2_b1_cls` in every respect.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k pretrained weights when
available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical PVT v2-B1 config.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.pvt import pvt_tiny_cls
>>> model = pvt_tiny_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)

see_also.name: pvt_v2_b1_cls
see_also.description: The canonical factory this alias forwards to.

members.name: pvt_v2_b0
members.extended:
Builds the smallest PVT v2 variant: `embed_dims=(32, 64, 160, 256)`,
`depths=(2, 2, 2, 2)`, `num_heads=(1, 2, 5, 8)`,
`sr_ratios=(8, 4, 2, 1)`.  Approximately **3.7M parameters** —
targeted at mobile / edge deployments.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k pretrained weights when
available in the model zoo.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical PVT v2-B0 config.

returns.description:
A `PVT` backbone returning a flat $(B, 256)$
feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.pvt import pvt_v2_b0
>>> model = pvt_v2_b0()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> feat = model.forward_features(x)
>>> feat.shape
(1, 256)
notes:
PVT v2-B0 reaches **70.5% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224 (Wang
et al., 2022, Table 1).  See
[arXiv:2106.13797](https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.13797).

members.name: pvt_v2_b0_cls
members.signature: pvt_v2_b0_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: PVTv2B0Weights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: PVT v2-B0 image classifier (Wang et al., 2022).
members.extended:
Combines the `pvt_v2_b0` backbone with a mean pool + single
`nn.Linear` classification head.  Default output is
`num_classes=1000` (ImageNet-1k).  ~3.7M parameters.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`PVTv2B0Weights.IN1K`); a tag
string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive with
`weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical PVT v2-B0 config.

returns.description:
Classifier returning `ImageClassificationOutput` whose
`logits` has shape `(B, num_classes)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.pvt import pvt_v2_b0_cls
>>> model = pvt_v2_b0_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
PVT v2-B0 reaches **70.5% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224 (Wang
et al., 2022, Table 1).  Pretrained weights are converted from
timm's `pvt_v2_b0.in1k` and hosted under `lucid-dl/pvt-v2-b0`.

members.name: pvt_v2_b1
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *PVT v2-B1* configuration:
`embed_dims=(64, 128, 320, 512)`, `depths=(2, 2, 2, 2)`,
`num_heads=(1, 2, 5, 8)`, `sr_ratios=(8, 4, 2, 1)`.
Approximately **14.0M parameters** — ResNet-18 / 34 class on the
accuracy/cost trade-off.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k pretrained weights when
available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical PVT v2-B1 config.

returns.description:
A `PVT` backbone returning a flat $(B, 512)$
feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.pvt import pvt_v2_b1
>>> model = pvt_v2_b1()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 512)
notes:
PVT v2-B1 reaches **78.7% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224 (Wang
et al., 2022, Table 1).

`embed_dims=(64, 128, 320, 512)`, `depths=(3, 4, 18, 3)` (a
deeper stage 3 than B2).  Approximately **45.2M parameters** —
ResNet-101 class on accuracy/cost.

members.name: pvt_v2_b5
members.extended:
Builds the largest PVT v2 variant *B5*:
`embed_dims=(64, 128, 320, 512)`, `depths=(3, 6, 40, 3)`,
and `mlp_ratios=(4.0, 4.0, 4.0, 4.0)` (uniform MLP ratio).
Approximately **82.9M parameters** — the largest variant in the
paper.

(1, 512)

PVT v2-B5 reaches **83.8% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224 (Wang
et al., 2022, Table 1) — the headline result of the paper.

members.name: PVTv2B2Weights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from timm's
`pvt_v2_b2.in1k` (Wang et al., 2022; ~25.4M params, 82.0% top-1).

`pvt_v2_b3.in1k` (Wang et al., 2022; ~45.2M params, 83.1% top-1).

summary: PVT — Pyramid Vision Transformer v2 (Wang et al., 2021/2022).
citation: Wang, Wenhai, et al. "Pyramid Vision Transformer: A Versatile Backbone for Dense Prediction without Convolutions." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision, 2021, pp. 568-578.
theory:
The Pyramid Vision Transformer (PVT) introduces a hierarchical
feature pyramid into a *pure-transformer* backbone so that it can
serve as a drop-in replacement for ResNet-style backbones in dense
prediction tasks such as detection and segmentation.  Plain ViT
produces a single-scale, low-resolution feature map and scales
quadratically with the input resolution; PVT addresses both
problems with two design changes.

First, the backbone has four stages, each beginning with a
*patch embedding* convolution (overlapping $7 \times 7$ in
PVT v2, non-overlapping in v1) of stride $s_i \in \{4, 2, 2, 2\}$, producing the canonical
$(\tfrac{H}{4}, \tfrac{W}{4}) \to \dots \to (\tfrac{H}{32}, \tfrac{W}{32})$ pyramid.  Second, each transformer
block uses *Spatial-Reduction Attention* (SRA): before computing
keys and values, the token sequence is reshaped back to a 2-D feature
map and downsampled by a stride-$R_i$ convolution, then
flattened again.  Concretely,



$$
K, V \leftarrow \mathrm{LN}\!\bigl(
    W_R \cdot \mathrm{Reshape}_{R_i \times R_i}(x)\bigr),
\qquad
\mathrm{SRA}(x) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(
    \frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right) V,
$$

where $R_i$ decreases with depth (e.g. $8, 4, 2, 1$).
This cuts the per-stage attention cost from
$\mathcal{O}((HW)^2)$ to $\mathcal{O}((HW)^2 / R_i^2)$,
enabling high-resolution inputs at reasonable cost.  Variants
PVT-Tiny / Small / Medium / Large scale stage depths along this
shared pyramidal-SRA design.
